data.title: GameStop CEO Calls For 'Owner's Mentality' In Corporate America — Says 'Risking Your Own Bottom Line Is The Only Thing That Keeps A Business Honest'
data.description:
Corporate America often rewards executives while shielding them from the risks of their decisions, according to GameStop Corp. (NYSE:GME) CEO Ryan Cohen.

"Risking your own bottom line is the only thing that keeps a business honest," he wrote on X in February.

Cohen criticized what he called a culture of "risk-free insiders" in corporate leadership and focused his message on accountability in corporate boardrooms. He urged executives to adopt an owner's mentality and treat shareholder money like their own.

Don't Miss:

‘The Hollow Men Of The Boardroom'

Cohen directed his criticism at three groups he said weaken corporate accountability. He began with independent directors who hold well-paid board seats yet rarely challenge management. The risk of losing those seats discourages confrontation, he wrote.

Cohen also criticized consultants. Companies often hire expensive advisory firms during major strategic decisions, he said. When those plans fail, responsibility can fall on the advisers.

Cohen grouped these figures together and labeled them the "hollow men of the boardroom."
"They wear the right suits" and "say the right buzzwords," he wrote, yet they carry little financial risk.

Buffett's Long Fight For Skin In The Game

Buffett wrote in his 1999 letter to shareholders that managers should run companies "as if these are the sole asset of their families and will remain so for the next century."

He has also criticized how corporate boards sometimes favor agreeable directors over those willing to challenge management.

"When seeking directors, CEOs don’t look for pit bulls," Buffett wrote in Berkshire's 2019 shareholder letter. "It’s the cocker spaniel that gets taken home."

He ties his philosophy to personal risk, with the majority of his net worth in Berkshire stock.

"We want to make money only when our partners do and in exactly the same proportion," Buffett wrote in Berkshire Hathaway's so-called owner's manual in 1996.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: $1000 Invested In Cameco 10 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:
Cameco (NYSE:CCJ) has outperformed the market over the past 10 years by 11.81% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 24.41%. Currently, Cameco has a market capitalization of $50.12 billion.

Buying $1000 In CCJ: If an investor had bought $1000 of CCJ stock 10 years ago, it would be worth $9,283.41 today based on a price of $115.08 for CCJ at the time of writing.

Cameco's Performance Over Last 10 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.
